title: Surround
subtitle: {key:ds}, {key:cs}, {key:ys} — operate on surroundings as a first-class concept.
summary: Tim Pope's vim-surround adds three operators — {key:y}{key:s} (add), {key:d}{key:s} (delete), {key:c}{key:s} (change) — plus *visual mode* {key:S}. The trailing key names the surrounding kind: quotes, brackets, tags, or function calls. Once you have it, it feels like core Vim.

blocks.text:
Vim's text-object grammar already lets you talk about the *content* inside surroundings — {key:i}{key:"}, {key:i}{key:(}, {key:i}{key:t} all select what's *between* the delimiters. But the delimiters themselves — the quotes, the brackets, the tags — are second-class: you can land your cursor on one with a motion, but you can't directly *operate* on "the thing wrapped around this word."

Tim Pope's [vim-surround](https://github.com/tpope/vim-surround) plugin closes that gap. It adds three operators that treat *surroundings* as first-class targets, completing the grammar. It's the plugin most often cited as "this should ship with Vim."

Every surround command is one sentence with two slots:

  *operator* + *kind*

The **operator** says what to do. The **kind** is a single key naming the surrounding — {key:'}, {key:"}, {key:(}, {key:[}, {key:{}, {key:<}, {key:t} (tag), {key:f} (function call). The {key:c}{key:s} operator takes *two* kinds because changing requires both an old and a new wrapper.

rows: With a selection already made in *visual mode*, press {key:S} then *kind* to wrap the selection.

blocks.text: {key:y}{key:s} is the only operator that takes a *motion* (or text object) before the *kind* — the motion picks what gets wrapped. {key:d}{key:s} and {key:c}{key:s} need no motion: they search outward from the cursor and operate on the *nearest enclosing* surround.

The *kind* key names a category of wrapper. Quotes are their own pair. Bracket pairs have two flavors: the *opener* form ({key:(}, {key:[}, {key:{}, {key:<}) adds a space on the inside; the *closer* form ({key:)}, {key:]}, {key:}}, {key:>}) does not. Tags and function calls *prompt* for the missing piece (tag name, function name).

rows: Self-pairing — same character on both sides.
{key:(} adds spaces inside: `( foo )`. {key:)} does not: `(foo)`.
Opener form adds spaces; closer form does not.
Plain `<…>` — *not* a tag, just the symbols.
Prompts: type `em>` (or `a href="...">`) and press Enter. Closing tag generated. {key:T} keeps the full opening tag on change.

blocks.text: The spaces-inside rule is mnemonic: an opening bracket *opens its arms* and leaves room; a closing bracket *closes tight*. If you remember which way the bracket is pointing, you remember whether you'll get spaces.

rows: wraps the selection in single quotes

blocks.text: Some shortcuts and variants follow the same operator-doubling pattern as core Vim ({key:d}{key:d}, {key:y}{key:y}):

rows: Surround the **whole current line** (no motion needed).
Like {key:y}{key:s} but place content on its **own indented lines**.
From an existing *visual mode* selection, {key:g}{key:S} wraps it like {key:S} but puts the content on its own lines.

blocks.title: Repeat Works
blocks.text: All three operators integrate with Tim Pope's [repeat.vim](https://github.com/tpope/vim-repeat), so {key:.} repeats the last surround command. {key:c}{key:s}{key:'}{key:"} on one string, then {key:n} to find the next single-quoted string, then {key:.} to convert it too. The grammar composes with the rest of Vim, exactly like a built-in operator.

blocks.title: Why It Belongs in Core
blocks.text:
Vim's grammar already understands the *content* inside surroundings — {key:i}{key:"}, {key:i}{key:(}, {key:i}{key:t}, etc. select the inner content. Surround completes the grammar by treating the *delimiters themselves* as a first-class concept that can be deleted, changed, or added.

The operator names are mnemonic: **d**elete-**s**urrounding, **c**hange-**s**urrounding, **y**ank-**s**urrounding ("surround with"). The plugin makes surroundings as natural to manipulate as words or lines — which is why most Vim users add it day one.

blocks.title: Where You Already Have It
blocks.text:
Surround is so universally adopted that it ships baked into several Vim emulations:

  - **VS Code Vim extension** (vscodevim/Vim) — surround commands ({key:y}{key:s}, {key:c}{key:s}, {key:d}{key:s}) work out of the box, no separate plugin needed. Enable with `"vim.surround": true` (default on).
  - **IdeaVim** (JetBrains IDEs) — bundled as the IdeaVim-Surround plugin, one click to install.
  - **Vim** and **Neovim** (the actual editors) — *not* built in. You still need a plugin. The two living maintained options are [`tpope/vim-surround`](https://github.com/tpope/vim-surround) (the original, works in both) and [`kylechui/nvim-surround`](https://github.com/kylechui/nvim-surround) (Lua rewrite for Neovim, slightly different defaults — see Strategy notes in the codebase). Some users prefer [`echasnovski/mini.surround`](https://github.com/echasnovski/mini.surround) as part of the `mini.nvim` collection.

Neovim has discussed adopting a built-in surround module several times but as of Neovim 0.10/0.11 it remains a plugin choice.
